title: Chapter 24: The Celestial Hall
paragraphs: Death in a fading world is a commonplace occurrence. It hides in the radioactive mist, lurks in the mandibles of monsters, and settles as gray dust on the lips of exhausted refugees. Usually, it comes unnoticed, erasing lives as the wind erases footprints on the sand.
But the death of the goblin from the Rotten Hollow stopped time.

The silence that descended upon the Gate of Lamentation was heavy, monumental. No one uttered a word when, from under the vaults of the Great Agdash, breaking all thousand-year taboos, High Elder Elarion emerged.

He left his sterile marble tomb of the White Hall. The elder walked on foot, without a retinue, his silver-embroidered robes dragging in the mud, absorbing the gray ash of the wastelands. Elarion approached the crater where elven guards, a weeping giant demon, a werewolf, and a fox stood on their knees.

The High Elder stopped before Yashil's body. The great elf, whose pride for centuries dictated laws to the continent, slowly raised trembling hands to his head. He removed his tiara—the crown of a ruler, forged from pure starry metal—and, dropping to his knees, carefully placed it on the frozen stone at the bare feet of the little goblin.
It was the end of an era. The wall of hatred that divided nations collapsed not by magic and not by a sword strike. It was broken by a heart that refused to hate.

Rene approached the body. The fox, whose yellow eyes were red from tears, gracefully crouched down. She took out her last, untouched mint cigarette from a secret pocket of her corset. She flicked her silver lighter, took one deep drag, and placed the smoldering cigarette next to the Elder's tiara. The smell of tart tobacco mixed with the smell of ash.
"Your move, Green Philosopher," Rene whispered, barely audible. "You won. We are not afraid anymore."

Dzhanavar approached next. The huge werewolf, whose fur was matted with the blood of Reapers, dropped onto all fours. He did not utter any quotes. All his fake lazy bravado vanished, revealing the true, ancient nature of a beast that had lost its leader. Dzhanavar tilted his massive muzzle to the leaden, rotting heavens and let out a long, drawn-out, soul-tearing wolf howl. There was no defiance in it—within was pure, undiluted grief. And when the howl subsided, the werewolf quietly, with infinite respect, exhaled into the void: "Augh, brother. Sleep peacefully."

Lord Valtor observed them from afar. The immortal vampire, whose heart for centuries remained a piece of cold ice, slowly placed a black-gloved hand to his chest and bowed low, respectfully. He turned and dissolved into the frosty mist, forever carrying in his eternal memory the lesson taught to him by a mortal.
For Yashil, pain no longer existed.

He opened his eyes. The first thing he realized was that the smell of rotting ozone and sulfur had vanished. The air filling his lungs was unimaginably clean. It smelled of morning dew, pine needles, and spring. The very smell that the goblin had only guessed at, looking from afar at the robes of passing elven caravans.

His body was light. His chest was no longer compressed by the weight of broken bones, and the wound on his cheekbone had vanished, leaving not even a scar. He was in the same simple, coarse burlap, but now it seemed woven of soft, warm light.
Yashil stood on a path paved not with stone, but with shimmering, transparent mana. Around him there was neither ash nor wastelands.

He was in the Celestial Hall—a place where the forces of the universe carefully preserved the memory of the greatest souls of all bygone eras. This was not a realm of gods; it was an echo of the universe itself, which had woven an abode for those whose spirit had transcended matter.

Ahead rose colossal arches of interwoven light. Under their vaults, Yashil saw figures. Hundreds, thousands of tall, translucent silhouettes. Spirits of ancient heroes. The first kings of the High Elves in shining armor, legendary gnome smiths, creators of the first runes, great sages and protectors of the world who had long since become myths.
They stood along the path, looking silently at the tiny green-skinned figure that had appeared in their sacred precincts.

Yashil stopped timidly. All his life he had known his place. He was a goblin. Wasteland filth. He was not allowed to enter the cities of the high peoples, let alone the Hall of Legends. Instinct told him to lower his head and turn off the path into the shadow.
But the spirits did not look away.

From the shining crowd, a tall, majestic silhouette stepped forward. It was the spirit of the very first founder of Agdash, the one whose name was carved on the foundation of the Great Gates. The ancient elven king approached the little goblin. His face, emitting light, was full of absolute reverence.
The great king of the past did not unsheathe a sword. He slowly dropped to one knee, bowing his head before the creature from the Rotten Hollow.

"You carried on your fragile shoulders what broke our steel-clad backs, Yashil," the voice of the ancient spirit sounded like the chiming of crystal bells, filling the Hall with warmth. "We built walls to protect bodies. You destroyed walls to save souls."

One by one, the spirits of the greatest heroes of bygone eras—elves, gnomes, ancient humans—stepped aside, clearing a path for the goblin to the very center of the Celestial Hall, to where the empty pedestals of true legends stood. They bowed their heads in a sign of deepest recognition. In death there were no longer races. There was no contempt. There was only absolute, crystal truth.
Yashil walked slowly along the path of light. He felt no pride, no triumph. He was simply happy.

At the very end of the hall, at the foot of a golden tree woven of pure mana, stood a female figure. She was short, with green skin and eyes full of infinite, all-forgiving love. She was no longer emaciated from hunger. She glowed with the same quiet, beautiful power as the greatest kings.
"Mama..." Yashil exhaled.

The little goblin rushed to her, and she dropped to her knees, holding her son tightly, forever. The tears that he had accumulated in himself for so long in the cruel world of the living shed in the world of spirits as a light, purifying rain.
He had returned home. And in the Celestial Hall, on par with the greatest heroes of the universe, the goblin whose weapon—kindness—proved stronger than the end of the world, forever took his place.
The world of the living continued its slow, agonizing decay.
The heavens were still sick, and the Void continued to grind the boundaries of reality. But in Agdash, something had changed irrevocably.

At the Gate of Lamentation, on the site of the smoking crater, elven stonemasons had erected a statue. It was not elegant or covered in gold. It was carved from simple, coarse basalt. A small figure of a goblin, leaning on an iron pipe and looking straight into the ashen sky. At the base of the statue, a small fire always burned.

His rest was guarded. A huge black demon, whose body was covered in acid scars, sat in the shadow of the buttresses, making sure that no creature of the Abyss approached this place. At night a lone wolf howled on the parapet, and in the mornings a fresh cigarette, emitting a minty aroma, invariably appeared at the stone feet of the statue.
Far from Agdash, through the gray mist and howling wind of the wastelands, a lone traveler trudged.
Avotu walked heavily, his armor clinking quietly with his steps. He was alone in a dead world. But his back was straight. His hand rested confidently on the hilt of the Blade of Nature.

The black flame writhing above the great elf's pale face no longer carried threat or cold. In its very core, where previously only icy despair had lived, a golden spark now burned steadily, brightly, and indomitably. It was a light given to him by the little goblin.
Avotu closed his eyes, tilting his face to the icy wind. The guilt that had choked him for centuries over Biotrix's death had given way to quiet peace. Grief is just love that has nowhere to go.

He carried this love within himself. He was ready to be a shield. And he would walk through this rotting eternity until space tore open again. Until five terrified people, having no magic but capable, like Yashil, of standing back to back in the darkness, fell to him from a foreign, distant world.
The goblin's story had concluded, becoming a legend.
The story of the Isekaiers and the Black Flame was only just beginning.
